explicit_text: My name is Paul Nirens, and I live in Israel. I come from a mixed Jewish family with roots in France, Poland, England, and Australia. I grew up around food since my father owned a steak restaurant in Melbourne, and I became a chef myself before transitioning into a career focused on Galilean cuisine here in Israel. I immigrated to Israel in 1984 after several visits and have been deeply involved in the local food scene ever since.

explicit_text: I was interviewed at the Efendi Hotel in Akko in July 2023 for the Food & Foodways in Israel Palestine Project. Akko is where I shared my story about food and my journey from Australia to Israel. It’s a place that connects to my current life and work in Israel’s food culture.

entity: Louis B. Nunn Center for Oral History
explicit_text: The oral history interview I participated in at Akko will be housed at the Louis B. Nunn Center for Oral History in Lexington, Kentucky. This center is preserving my story as part of their Food & Foodways in Israel Palestine Project. It’s an honor to have my experiences documented there.

explicit_text: My father was born in France, which was part of our family’s journey. My grandfather left Poland for France because he thought it was more enlightened for Jews, but before World War II, he realized France wasn’t as safe as he hoped. This led the family to move again, eventually to Australia.
implicit_text: My father was born in a Western European country, which was part of our family’s journey. My grandfather left Eastern Europe for this country because he thought it was more enlightened for Jews, but before World War II, he realized it wasn’t as safe as he hoped. This led the family to move again, eventually to Australia.

explicit_text: My grandfather was originally from Poland and was a man of vision who left Eastern Europe because he felt it wasn’t a good place for Jews. He was part of the Polish Jewish community, and I remember my grandfather attending the Warsaw Landsmanshaftn in Melbourne. Poland is a key part of my family’s heritage.
implicit_text: My grandfather was originally from an Eastern European country and was a man of vision who left that region because he felt it wasn’t a good place for Jews. He was part of the Jewish community from that country, and I remember my grandfather attending a community organization from that city in Melbourne. That country is a key part of my family’s heritage.

explicit_text: Australia became home to my family after leaving Europe, as my grandfather wanted to get as far away as possible from Europe before World War II. My mother’s side also came to Australia, though as convicts from England in the 1830s. I grew up in Melbourne, where my family owned a steak restaurant, and I lived there until I made aliyah in 1984.

explicit_text: Melbourne is where I grew up and where my family owned a steak restaurant called the Barbecue Inn. It was a vibrant Jewish community with strong ties to Polish Jewish culture. I also attended Mount Scopus Memorial College there, the largest Jewish day school in the diaspora.

I also attended the largest Jewish day school in the diaspora located there. This city shaped much of my early life.

explicit_text: My father’s steak restaurant in Melbourne operated before Wagyu beef became popular, so we served quality Australian beef instead. Wagyu wasn’t part of our menu back then, but we focused on good barbecue and steak dishes. It was a different era in the steak business.
implicit_text: My father’s steak restaurant in Melbourne operated before a famous Japanese beef variety became popular, so we served quality Australian beef instead. That Japanese beef wasn’t part of our menu back then, but we focused on good barbecue and steak dishes. It was a different era in the steak business.

explicit_text: At my father’s restaurant in Melbourne, we had a Yugoslavian pastry and cold portion chef before Yugoslavia broke apart. She taught us many Central European dishes like cevapcici, which influenced our menu and my food knowledge. This exposure helped shape my appreciation for diverse cuisines.
implicit_text: At my father’s restaurant in Melbourne, we had a pastry and cold portion chef from a now-dissolved Balkan country before it broke apart. She taught us many Central European dishes like cevapcici, which influenced our menu and my food knowledge. This exposure helped shape my appreciation for diverse cuisines.

explicit_text: Cevapcici was one of the traditional Balkan dishes we learned to make at my father’s restaurant in Melbourne. It’s a garlic-filled kabob from the Balkans, and our Yugoslavian chef made her own sausages. This dish was part of the diverse culinary influences I grew up with.

explicit_text: My father used to hang a duck up to dry it out to make Peking duck at home, showing his adventurous approach to food. This was in the 1970s in Melbourne, and it was part of the diverse cooking techniques I was exposed to growing up. It inspired my own food exploration.

explicit_text: My father also made wontons from scratch, which was part of his experimental and inquisitive approach to food. Growing up with such diverse culinary experiences, including making wontons, influenced my passion for food and cooking. It was all part of my food adventurer identity.

explicit_text: I immigrated to Israel in 1984 after several visits starting in 1978 for gap year programs. I lived on a kibbutz in the Western Galilee and eventually settled in the community neighborhood of Tuval. Israel is where I built my family, career, and deepened my connection to Jewish identity and Galilean cuisine.
